summary:
The Early Helladic Period, approximately 3000–2000 BC, represents the first Bronze Age phase on the Greek mainland, characterised by the spread of settled agricultural communities, the adoption of bronze metallurgy, growing social differentiation, and expanding maritime trade networks in the Aegean. Early Helladic settlements were distributed across the Peloponnese, Boeotia, Attica, and other regions of mainland Greece, and they participated in a wider Aegean exchange network connecting them to the Cycladic islands and western Anatolia.

Early Helladic society is primarily known from archaeological evidence rather than written records, and it reflects a world of relatively small communities organised around agriculture, craft production, and local trade. Characteristic pottery styles — especially the 'sauceboat' form of the Early Helladic II phase, which has been found at sites across the Aegean — attest to shared cultural connections across a wide area. Some sites, such as Lerna in the Argolid, show evidence of more complex organisation, including large rectangular buildings ('House of the Tiles') that may have served administrative functions.

The Early Helladic Period ended around 2100–2000 BC with widespread site destructions and abandonments across mainland Greece, probably associated with the arrival of new population groups — likely the ancestors of the later Greek-speaking Hellenic peoples. This transition marks the beginning of the Middle Helladic Period and sets the stage for the eventual emergence of [[event:57|Mycenaean civilisation]] in the second millennium BC. The Early Helladic Period thus represents the deep prehistoric foundations upon which classical Greek civilisation would ultimately be built.
